description: Disposing a signal disposes only its eager puller. The backing value survives, stays readable, stays CORRECT, and reverts to lazy recompute-on-read. This is normative clause 4 of *Signal eagerness* (reactive-graph.md), and it is the reason the operation every binding spells `dispose_signal` is a naming inaccuracy rather than a description -- it does not dispose the signal, it de-eagers it. A caller who trusts the name expects teardown and gets a still-live lazy memo. This fixture pins three failures that a values-only assertion cannot separate. (a) A binding that disposes the backing slot along with the puller makes the value unreadable, so step 3 errors instead of returning. (b) A binding that disposes the puller but leaves the value FROZEN at its last eager materialization returns the pre-write value at step 4 -- correct-looking if you only ever check that a read succeeds, and wrong. (c) A binding whose dispose is a no-op on the puller keeps re-materializing eagerly, which step 3 catches on the compute count while every value in the fixture stays identical to the conforming case. The steps are ordered so that (c) is caught BEFORE any read, because a read would mask it by triggering the lazy recompute that makes the counts agree again. Contract: #lzsignaleager clause 4.

expect.note: disposal removes the puller and nothing else. The value must remain readable — a binding that tears down the backing slot here fails (a). Disposal itself must not compute.

THE discriminating step, and deliberately carries no read. The puller is gone, so an invalidating write must NOT re-materialize. A binding whose dispose did not actually remove the puller shows 2 here — and every value in this fixture is identical either way, so this count is the only thing that separates them. Ordered before any read because a read would trigger the lazy recompute and make both cases show 2.

the value survived AND is correct: lazy recompute-on-read observed the write. A binding that froze the value at disposal returns 11 here, which is failure (b) — readable, plausible, and stale forever.
the de-eagered signal is an ordinary memo: the recompute is cached, so a second read does not recompute
